stage.name: Source-to-slide practice: turn evidence into a lesson
stage.description: A production-method lesson for future course generation from source evidence.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: Good slides are compressed evidence, not decoration

content.title: Good slides are compressed evidence, not decoration
content.subtitle: A micro-lesson should preserve claim, source, scope, caveat, and learner action.
content.thesis: The source-to-slide workflow is how this course should keep improving.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 documentation README
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 documentation is generated from repository material, including configuration-specific instruction and design manuals.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson is Source-to-slide practice: turn evidence into a lesson. A micro-lesson should preserve claim, source, scope, caveat, and learner action. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

actions.text: This lesson is Source-to-slide practice: turn evidence into a lesson. A micro-lesson should preserve claim, source, scope, caveat, and learner action. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

content.title: One source claim becomes four artifacts
content.subtitle: The lesson package should keep each artifact editable.

cards.title: Evidence slice
cards.body: Source anchor, local path, exact section, and claim.

cards.title: Slide
cards.body: Visual structure that compresses the claim.

cards.title: Narration
cards.body: AlexChen explains the reasoning and caveats.

cards.title: Quiz
cards.body: Choice-only check that catches common misreadings.

content.takeaway: A lesson is a reproducible evidence transform.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 documentation README
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 documentation is generated from repository material, including configuration-specific instruction and design manuals.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: scope and verification intent
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document defines the CVA6 feature scope and ties requirements to verification intent and interface requirements.

content.speakerNotes: This is the production workflow behind the classroom. Start with a source-backed claim. Turn it into an evidence slice, then a slide, then narration, then a quiz. If the learner or operator changes the claim, the artifacts can be regenerated. That is why each standalone lesson package has editable material and final release material.

actions.text: This is the production workflow behind the classroom. Start with a source-backed claim. Turn it into an evidence slice, then a slide, then narration, then a quiz. If the learner or operator changes the claim, the artifacts can be regenerated. That is why each standalone lesson package has editable material and final release material.

scenes.title: A claim must be small enough to test

content.title: Do not put a whole subsystem into one sentence

items.title: Narrow
items.body: One behavior, one boundary, one source-backed conclusion.

items.title: Scoped
items.body: Configuration, branch, and document context are explicit.

items.title: Testable
items.body: A quiz can distinguish correct understanding from a common wrong shortcut.

content.takeaway: If the claim is too big, the lesson will become vague.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: scope and verification intent
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document defines the CVA6 feature scope and ties requirements to verification intent and interface requirements.

content.speakerNotes: A vague slide usually starts with a vague claim. For example, CVA6 supports interfaces is too broad. CVA6 uses AXI as a memory interface in specific configurations is better. It has a source, a boundary, and a testable wrong answer. That is how we avoid empty narration.

actions.text: A vague slide usually starts with a vague claim. For example, CVA6 supports interfaces is too broad. CVA6 uses AXI as a memory interface in specific configurations is better. It has a source, a boundary, and a testable wrong answer. That is how we avoid empty narration.

scenes.title: Operator review is part of the process

content.title: High-quality courses need human editorial judgment
content.subtitle: Automation handles structure; Alex chooses emphasis and public taste.

columns.title: Automation handles
columns.body: Package structure, source anchors, slide skeleton, audio regeneration, and publishing sync.

columns.title: Operator handles
columns.body: Topic priority, technical emphasis, final wording, and public release approval.

content.takeaway: The workflow is assisted production, not blind generation.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 public repository source tree
sourceAnchors.locator: repository directories core, docs, corev_apu, verif, config, util
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The public repository is organized around core RTL, documentation, APU/testharness material, verification, configuration, and tooling directories.

content.speakerNotes: This is also a statement of how we should build OpenHW Explorer. Codex can generate, package, check, and publish. Alex should still decide emphasis, language, and final public quality. That hybrid process is more likely to produce a serious technical brand than either pure automation or pure manual production.

actions.text: This is also a statement of how we should build OpenHW Explorer. Codex can generate, package, check, and publish. Alex should still decide emphasis, language, and final public quality. That hybrid process is more likely to produce a serious technical brand than either pure automation or pure manual production.

content.speakerNotes: This quiz checks the core judgment of Source-to-slide practice: turn evidence into a lesson. The right answers should feel less like trivia and more like disciplined classification: what is the boundary, what is the source, what is the limitation, and what would we check next?

actions.text: This quiz checks the core judgment of Source-to-slide practice: turn evidence into a lesson. The right answers should feel less like trivia and more like disciplined classification: what is the boundary, what is the source, what is the limitation, and what would we check next?

scenes.title: Summary: Source-to-slide practice: turn evidence into a lesson

content.title: Source-to-slide practice: turn evidence into a lesson
takeaways: Transform a source-backed technical claim into a maintainable OpenHW Explorer micro-lesson unit.
Turn every public explanation into an evidence-backed learning artifact.
content.nextStep: Next: verification handoff, where architecture reading becomes testable engineering questions.
content.closing: This is how OpenHW Explorer can teach serious open hardware without drifting into unsupported certainty.

content.speakerNotes: To summarize: Transform a source-backed technical claim into a maintainable OpenHW Explorer micro-lesson unit. Keep the claim small, keep the source visible, and keep the boundary honest. Next: verification handoff, where architecture reading becomes testable engineering questions.
